steps.reveal: Factum est = it happened (Hebraism in the Vulgate)

hints.perfect passive 'it was made': 'Factum est' can mean 'was made,' but here it is the narrative formula 'it came to pass'
hints.result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' — this is an impersonal construction meaning 'it happened'
hints.passive periphrastic: Passive periphrastic uses the gerundive — 'factum' here is a perfect participle

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'Factum est' translates to?
steps.target: to make / to do (here: to happen)
choices: to make / to do (here: to happen)

hints.to be: 'To be' is 'esse' — 'factum est' comes from 'facere/fieri' (to make/become)

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'Factum est' here?

hints.present indicative: Present of 'fieri' would be 'fit' — 'factum est' is perfect
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'factum erat' — 'est' makes this perfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'fiebat' — 'factum est' is perfect

hints.fio, fieri, factus sum: 'Fieri' serves as the passive of 'facere' — both share the 4th principal part 'factum'
hints.fallo, fallere, fefelli, falsum: These are the parts of 'fallere' (to deceive)
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugere' (to flee)

hints.but: 'Autem' can mean 'but' in some contexts, but here in narrative it is a mild connective 'now'
hints.therefore: 'Therefore' is 'igitur' or 'ergo' — 'autem' is transitional, not inferential
hints.although: 'Although' is 'quamquam' or 'etsi' — 'autem' is a mild connective

hints.causal clause: 'Cum' + subjunctive can be causal, but here it sets the temporal scene: 'when all the people were being baptized'
hints.concessive clause: 'Cum' + subjunctive can be concessive ('although'), but context is temporal here
hints.preposition 'with': 'Cum' as preposition takes ablative — here it introduces a clause with a subjunctive verb

hints.to wash: 'To wash' is 'lavare' — 'baptizare' is a Greek loanword specifically meaning to baptize
hints.to immerse (secular): While Greek 'baptizein' means immerse, in Vulgate Latin 'baptizare' is the sacramental term

steps.reveal: baptizaretur = was being baptized (impf. pass. subjv.)

hints.present passive subjunctive: Present passive subjunctive would be 'baptizetur' — 'baptizaretur' has the -re- of imperfect
hints.pluperfect passive subjunctive: Pluperfect passive subjunctive would be 'baptizatus esset' — 'baptizaretur' is imperfect
hints.imperfect passive indicative: Imperfect passive indicative would be 'baptizabatur' — '-retur' marks the subjunctive

hints.lavo, lavare, lavi, lautum: These are the parts of 'lavare' (to wash)
hints.mergo, mergere, mersi, mersum: These are the parts of 'mergere' (to plunge/immerse)
hints.purgo, purgare, purgavi, purgatum: These are the parts of 'purgare' (to cleanse)

steps.reveal: omnis populus = all the people (nom., subject of 'baptizaretur')

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'omnem populum' — 'omnis populus' is nominative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'omnis populi' — 'populus' with -us is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'omni populo' — 'populus' is nominative subject

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'populi' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'populi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'populi' ends in -i

steps.prompt: Why is 'Factum est...cum' a characteristic Vulgate construction?
steps.target: it translates the Hebrew narrative formula 'wayhi...ke-' (and it happened when)
choices: it translates the Hebrew narrative formula 'wayhi...ke-' (and it happened when)
it is standard classical Latin prose style
it is a medieval Latin innovation

hints.it is standard classical Latin prose sty: Classical Latin rarely uses 'factum est' as a narrative formula — this reflects Semitic influence
hints.it imitates Greek aorist usage: While the Vulgate translates from Greek, 'factum est' reflects the underlying Hebrew 'wayhi'
hints.it is a medieval Latin innovation: This construction appears in Jerome's 4th-century translation, predating medieval Latin

translation: Now it happened when all the people were being baptized
